subject: ap-government
title: Argument Essay — Balance of Power Between the President and Congress
stimulus: The power of the executive branch in relation to the legislative branch has varied over time. Develop an argument that takes a position on the appropriate balance of power between the president and Congress.

parts.prompt: Respond to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis that establishes a line of reasoning. (Use at least one piece of evidence from one of the following foundational documents: Declaration of Independence, The Federalist 51, The Federalist 70.)
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row A — Thesis/Claim): Responds to the prompt with a defensible claim or thesis establishing a line of reasoning about the appropriate balance of power between the president and Congress.

Examples that earn the point: 'The power of the executive should be strengthened relative to Congress because Congress is slow to react and sometimes ends up in gridlock.' OR 'The power of Congress should be strengthened relative to the president because Congress is the representative of the will of the people.' OR 'The power of the executive and legislative branches should be equal because no one branch should become too powerful/limited government.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must take a position on the appropriate balance of power. Restating the prompt or simply noting that powers vary over time does not earn the point.

parts.prompt: Support your claim with at least TWO pieces of specific and relevant evidence. One piece of evidence must come from one of the foundational documents listed. A second piece of evidence can come from any other foundational document or from course concepts.
rubric_criteria: 0 pts: No accurate evidence, or evidence not relevant to the topic.
1 pt (Row B — 1 point): Provides one piece of evidence relevant to the topic.
2 pts (Row B — 2 points): One piece of specific and relevant evidence supports the claim (requires Row A).

3 pts (Row B — 3 points): Two pieces of specific and relevant evidence support the claim, one from a listed foundational document (Declaration of Independence, Federalist 51, or Federalist 70) (requires Row A).

Examples of acceptable evidence: 'Federalist 70 argues that a strong, unified executive brings energy and safety to the government.' 'Federalist 51 argues that ambition must counteract ambition to prevent any one branch from becoming too powerful.' 'The Declaration of Independence identifies the dangers of concentrated power.' 'The president has the power of executive order to act without Congress.' 'Congress has the power of the purse to check presidential power.'
parts.scoring_notes: To earn 2 or 3 points, must have earned Row A. To earn 3 points, one piece of evidence must come from the Declaration of Independence, Federalist 51, or Federalist 70.

parts.prompt: Use reasoning to explain why your evidence supports your claim or thesis.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row C — Reasoning): Explains the relationship between evidence and the claim. Must connect at least one piece of evidence to the argument.

Examples that earn the point: 'Federalist 70 supports a strong executive because, as Hamilton argues, unity in the executive allows for decisive action and clear accountability, which are essential for effective governance.' 'Federalist 51 supports limiting executive power because Madison argues that without institutional checks, ambition will lead to the consolidation of power and the loss of liberty.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A and at least 2 points in Row B. Listing evidence without explaining how it connects to the thesis does not earn this point.

parts.prompt: Respond to an opposing or alternate perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal.
rubric_criteria: 1 pt (Row D — Alternate Perspective): Responds to an opposing perspective using refutation, concession, or rebuttal. Must both describe an alternate perspective AND engage with it.

Examples that earn the point: 'Some people argue that the president should have more power because of efficiency, but concentrating power in the executive undermines the representative nature of government that Congress provides.' 'While some argue that Congress should be stronger because it is more representative, divided government often leads to gridlock that prevents effective policy making, which is why a stronger executive is necessary.'
parts.scoring_notes: Must have earned Row A. Must describe an alternate perspective AND refute, concede, or rebut it — not merely acknowledge it exists.
